source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Compare and group together different kinds of rocks on the basis of their appearance and simple physical properties; describe in simple terms how fossils are formed when things that have lived are trapped within rock; and recognise that soils are made from rocks and organic matter.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack uses observation-led virtual tests, layered fossil sequences and soil-component models. It distinguishes evidence-based grouping from rock-name memorisation and presents fossil formation as a long process without implying that every dead organism becomes a fossil.

objective.child_goal: I can compare rocks, explain a simple fossil sequence and describe what soil contains.
objective.statement: Observe, test, compare and group rocks by simple properties; explain how some fossils form in rock over long periods; and recognise soil as a mixture containing rock particles and organic matter.
misconceptions: Groups rocks only by colour or a memorised name and assumes all pieces of one named rock have identical appearance.
Uses 'hard', 'strong', 'heavy' and 'waterproof' as if they mean the same property or reports a test without keeping conditions consistent.
Thinks a fossil is the original whole organism placed inside a rock, or that fossils form quickly from every dead plant and animal.
Thinks soil is only crushed rock, only dirt, or a single uniform material without air, water or organic matter.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A feature of a material that can be observed or tested.
vocabulary.audio_script: A property is something we can observe or test, such as grain size, hardness or water absorption.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: One rock linked to labelled observation and test cards.

vocabulary.audio_script: A rock that absorbs water takes some water into its tiny spaces.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Equal drops placed on two rocks with different remaining puddles.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Preserved evidence of a living thing from long ago, often found in rock.
vocabulary.audio_script: A fossil can be a preserved remain, impression or trace of something that lived long ago.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A shell impression and a track trace shown inside rock layers.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Small pieces of material that settle in layers.
vocabulary.audio_script: Sediment can cover remains and build up in layers.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Sand and mud particles settling over a shell.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Material in soil that comes from living things or things that were once alive.
vocabulary.audio_script: Organic matter includes tiny pieces of decayed plants and animals.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Leaf fragments mixed with mineral particles in a soil sample.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A mixture of rock particles, organic matter, air and water.
vocabulary.audio_script: Soil is a mixture, and different soils contain different amounts of its parts.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A transparent jar showing settled layers, water and air spaces.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which observations describe what a material looks like, and which require a fair test?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate the distinction between observable features and tested properties.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: We can observe colour and grain size directly. We need a planned test for properties such as water absorption.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Cards for colour, grain, scratch result and absorbed drops sort into OBSERVE and TEST columns.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Inspect four rock samples and decide which evidence could be used to group them.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Frame classification as a decision based on stated properties rather than one correct naming system.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Scientists can group the same samples in different useful ways if they state and apply one rule consistently.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Four varied samples sit beside magnifier, scratch and drop-test tools.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Compare water absorption using the same number of drops, waiting time and sample area.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model a simple comparative test and precise property language.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Change the rock sample, keep the drop amount and waiting time the same, then compare the evidence.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two samples receive five equal drops and a timer before the remaining water is recorded.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Order the cards from a living organism to burial, more layers, rock formation and later discovery.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Build an age-appropriate causal sequence while avoiding instant-fossil and whole-organism models.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Some remains or traces are buried by sediment. Over a very long time, layers build and harden, and preserved evidence may remain.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A five-panel sequence shows a shell, burial, layered sediment, rock with an impression and exposed fossil.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A soil sample contains grains, leaf pieces, water and air spaces. Which claim about soil is best supported?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Detect single-substance soil models and connect observations to mixture language.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Name the different parts you can observe, then decide whether soil is one substance or a mixture.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A magnified soil cross-section has text-labelled mineral grains, organic fragments, water and air spaces.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion how rock evidence, fossil layers and soil components tell three different scientific stories.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Gather explanation evidence and keep observation, process and composition claims distinct.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: For each claim, point to the observation, test result or sequence step that supports it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three evidence frames are labelled COMPARE, SEQUENCE and MIXTURE.

manipulatives.purpose: Observe and compare rock samples using repeatable simple tests and learner-defined grouping rules.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners magnify texture and grains, use a simulated scratch comparison, apply equal water drops, record results and group samples by one or more stated properties.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Every image has a structured description of colour, grain and texture; tests produce numeric or categorical tables; tools work by buttons and keyboard; grouping can use a list instead of dragging; colour is never the only property; and close visual inspection has zoom and spoken alternatives.

manipulatives.purpose: Construct fossil-formation sequences and soil mixture models using ordered, labelled evidence.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners order event cards, inspect changing layers, compare trace and body fossils, then assemble soil samples from varied proportions of rock particles and organic matter.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Sequences have numbered text-card mode and explicit time language; layers use patterns plus labels; soil proportions have tables rather than colour-only jars; animations can be paused or stepped; and no task requires handling, tasting, breaking or collecting real specimens.

practice.interleaving_notes: Secure observation vocabulary before comparative tests. Teach fossils as an ordered process and soil as a mixture in separate models, then interleave only when the prompt clearly identifies whether the learner is comparing a property, explaining a process or describing composition.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Describe and group varied rock samples using explicit observable properties.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: three to six sample records + colour/grain/texture/layer data + learner-selected grouping rule + explain exceptions
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid implying that images identify rock type with certainty; allow multiple valid groups when the rule is stated and consistently applied.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare hardness and water absorption through simple controlled virtual tests.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two to four samples + one changed sample variable + equal test conditions + observation table + evidence-based comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: State that classroom scratch tests compare samples rather than assign absolute scientific hardness; verify all generated results and conclusions match.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Order and explain simple pathways by which remains, impressions or traces can be preserved in rock.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: organism or trace + burial by sediment + layer accumulation + long-time rock formation + erosion/exposure + sequence explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use 'some' and 'may' accurately, represent long time without a precise invented duration and distinguish original remains, impressions and traces.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Recognise soils as mixtures and compare samples using evidence about rock particles and organic matter.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two soil composition tables or labelled jar models + component identification + relative comparison + evidence statement
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not present one fixed recipe for all soils; distinguish organic matter from living organisms and acknowledge air and water without exceeding the objective.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Retain property, fossil-process and soil-mixture understanding through short evidence-linked review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue weighted to prior misconception + sample table, ordered card or soil diagram + one claim + one evidence choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep the scientific focus explicit, use non-visual alternatives and avoid unsafe instructions involving unknown dust, sharp samples or outdoor collection.

body.prompt: Group the samples using grain size. Which two belong together under the rule 'large visible grains'?

samples.description: smooth surface, grains too small to see

hints: Use the stated rule, not colour.
Find every sample whose description says the grains are large and visible.
question_variants.explanation: Samples A and C both have large visible grains. Their different colours do not change this grouping rule.

body.prompt: Each sample receives five equal drops for the same time. Sample P absorbs four drops and sample Q absorbs one. Which conclusion is supported?
choices: P absorbed more water than Q
Both rocks have every property in common

expected_answer.value: P absorbed more water than Q

hints: Use only the evidence produced by this test.
The test measured water absorption, not mass or hardness.
question_variants.explanation: Under the same conditions, P took in four drops and Q took in one, so P absorbed more water.

body.prompt: Put the fossil-formation events in the most sensible order.
events: More sediment builds up and the layers slowly form rock
A shell lies on the seabed
Erosion later exposes preserved evidence in the rock
Sediment quickly covers the shell or its impression

value: A shell lies on the seabed
Sediment quickly covers the shell or its impression
More sediment builds up and the layers slowly form rock
Erosion later exposes preserved evidence in the rock

hints: Begin before the shell is buried.
Discovery can happen only after preserved evidence is in rock and later exposed.
question_variants.explanation: Burial comes before layers build and harden over a very long time; later erosion may expose the preserved evidence.

body.prompt: A soil sample contains mineral grains, small decayed leaf pieces, water and air spaces. Which description is most accurate?
choices: Soil is a mixture containing rock particles and organic matter
Soil is made only from living plants

expected_answer.value: Soil is a mixture containing rock particles and organic matter

hints: List the different components named in the sample.
Decayed leaf pieces are organic matter.
question_variants.explanation: The sample contains different materials, including mineral rock particles and organic matter, so soil is a mixture.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Groups rocks only by colour or a memorised name and assumes all pieces of one named rock have identical appearance.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner ignores the stated property rule, names samples from images with certainty or rejects a valid alternative grouping.
repair_sequence: Describe each sample without naming it.
Choose one observable property and write a precise grouping rule.
Apply the same rule to every sample, then compare with a different valid rule.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner creates and consistently applies a property-based grouping to a fresh sample set.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses 'hard', 'strong', 'heavy' and 'waterproof' as if they mean the same property or reports a test without keeping conditions consistent.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner draws a conclusion about an untested property or changes drop amount, time or tool between samples.
repair_sequence: Name exactly which property the test measures.
Keep all conditions the same except the rock sample.
Record the observation, then write only the comparison supported by that evidence.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner plans or interprets a fair comparison and avoids claims about untested properties.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a fossil is the original whole organism placed inside a rock, or that fossils form quickly from every dead plant and animal.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner omits burial and long-term layering, describes instant transformation or says all remains become fossils.
repair_sequence: Separate the organism, remains, impression and trace possibilities.
Order burial, layer build-up, rock formation and later exposure.
Use 'some remains or traces may be preserved over a very long time' in the explanation.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains a new fossil sequence with conditional language and correct event order.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks soil is only crushed rock, only dirt, or a single uniform material without air, water or organic matter.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner selects one component as the whole soil or expects every sample to have identical proportions.
repair_sequence: Inspect or read a labelled soil sample description.
Sort components into rock particles, organic matter, water and air.
Compare two mixtures and explain that soils can contain different proportions.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner identifies soil as a mixture and supports a comparison with component evidence.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm field-lab background, one sample or layer sequence at a time, no scraping sound effects and no dusty particle animations.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace falling sediment, settling jars and erosion animations with numbered static layers and labelled before/after panels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Describe each sample's observable properties, announce test conditions and results, and narrate fossil events in chronological order with replay.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Pair property terms with icons and concise definitions, keep observation tables visible and use one causal claim per sentence.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Use fixed OBSERVE-TEST-RECORD-COMPARE and ORGANISM-BURY-LAYERS-ROCK-EXPOSE routines, changing one variable at a time.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, allow step-by-step sequences, preserve test data and provide list, table and text alternatives to spatial grouping.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat grouping rules and predictions as testable proposals, retain accurate observations during repair and distinguish an unsupported claim from a failed learner.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach rock, grain, property, absorb, sediment, fossil, soil, mixture and organic matter with real-object photographs, gestures and sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires at least 80% across property-based rock grouping, one comparative test, fossil sequence and soil-mixture recognition. Secure requires 90% with fair-test reasoning, conditional fossil language and evidence-based soil comparison on two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can compare rocks using evidence, explain a simple fossil-formation sequence and describe soil as a mixture.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Stores observations, grouping rule, controlled conditions, test result, sequence order and soil components separately so labels or memorised facts do not substitute for scientific reasoning.
next_actions: Route colour-only groups to multi-property observation and learner-defined rule practice.
Route unsupported test claims to variable-control and evidence-language repair.
Compare several fossil types after the core burial-and-layer sequence is secure.
Extend secure soil understanding into simple comparative investigations without tasting, breaking or inhaling samples.

qa.notes: Deep review-status Year 3 pack. Human review should verify sample-property claims, virtual test validity, classification acceptance, fossil sequence accuracy and time language, soil-component models, non-visual alternatives and specimen-safety wording before promotion.
